schools.summary: The inference from Christ's suffering (3:18): 'forasmuch then as Christ hath suffered for us in the flesh (in his body), arm yourselves likewise with the same mind (ennoian — the same purpose, resolve, disposition): for he that hath suffered in the flesh hath ceased from sin' — as soldiers take up weapons, believers to arm themselves with the same resolute mind Christ had (a readiness to suffer in doing God's will, the determination to suffer rather than sin, the figure of a warrior taking up arms for the conflict); 'he that hath suffered in the flesh hath ceased from sin' read either as one who has suffered for righteousness having, by that very willingness, broken with sin (the resolve to suffer rather than sin means one has done with sin), or as the believer who in union with Christ suffers (dies to the flesh) having ceased from sin (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Christ hath suffered for us in the flesh, arm yourselves likewise with the same mind
he that hath suffered in the flesh hath ceased from sin
the call to arm with the mind of Christ
the resolve to suffer rather than sin

schools.summary: The purpose of the armed mind (v.1): 'that he no longer should live the rest of his time in the flesh (the remainder of his earthly life) to the lusts of men (human passions, sinful desires), but to the will of God' — the goal of arming with Christ's mind being that the believer spend the rest of his life governed not by sinful human desires but by God's will; 'no longer' marking the decisive break with the old life of lust (the believer done with living for sinful passions, the old life left behind), and 'the will of God' the new rule and aim of the remaining life (obedience to God replacing obedience to lust, the transformed direction of the Christian life) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: that he no longer should live the rest of his time in the flesh to the lusts of men, but to the will of God
the new aim: to live to God's will, not to men's lusts
the decisive break with the old life of lust
God's will the new rule

schools.summary: The pointed (ironic) statement: 'for the time past of our life may suffice (is enough) us to have wrought (done, carried out) the will of the Gentiles (the heathen, the unconverted), when we walked in lasciviousness (sensuality), lusts (evil desires), excess of wine (drunkenness), revellings (carousing), banquetings (drinking parties), and abominable (unlawful, detestable) idolatries' — the past time spent in heathen sin more than enough, no need to give any more of life to such ways (ironic understatement); the catalogue listing the characteristic sins of the pagan life (especially sensuality and intemperance, the works of the flesh the readers had practised before conversion), crowned by 'abominable idolatries' (the worship of idols, often bound up with the immoral revelry just named), the whole marking the corrupt life from which the gospel had delivered them (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: the time past of our life may suffice us to have wrought the will of the Gentiles
lasciviousness, lusts, excess of wine, revellings, banquetings, and abominable idolatries
the past life of heathen sin is enough
the corrupt past the gospel delivered them from

schools.summary: The reaction of the former companions: 'wherein they think it strange (are surprised, find it odd) that ye run not with them to the same excess of riot (asotia — profligacy, debauchery, the same flood of dissipation, the wild rush of reckless living), speaking evil of you' — the heathen astonished that the believers no longer plunge with them into the same excesses (the converted having broken from the crowd), the believers' refusal to join the wild dissipation marking them out and provoking resentment; the offended heathen responding by slandering and reviling the Christians for their separation (the godly life rebuking the wicked, who retaliate with slander, the believer to expect such evil-speaking) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: they think it strange that ye run not with them to the same excess of riot, speaking evil of you
the heathen malign believers who no longer join their excess
godly separation provoking the world's resentment
the cost of breaking from the crowd

schools.summary: The answer to the slander: 'who shall give account (a reckoning) to him that is ready (prepared, at hand) to judge the quick (the living) and the dead' — the heathen slanderers having to answer to God (or Christ), who is ready to judge all, both the living and the dead (their evil-speaking to be reckoned with); the Judge 'ready' (prepared and at hand to judge, the judgment certain and near, the slanderers to remember they face a Judge); 'the quick and the dead' the comprehensive scope (all without exception, both those alive at the judgment and those already dead), the comfort of the persecuted (their slanderers will answer) and a solemn warning (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Who shall give account to him that is ready to judge the quick and the dead
the slanderers must give account to the Judge of living and dead
the certain and near reckoning
comfort for the persecuted, warning for the wicked

schools.summary: Barnes (with Wetstein, Rosenmuller, Bloomfield) holds 'them that are dead' are literally those now dead who heard the gospel while living and became Christians: the gospel was preached to them (when alive) so that, though 'judged according to men in the flesh' (condemned and put to death by men, as the martyrs were), they might 'live according to God in the spirit' (live to God in their souls, now and forever) — fitting the context, an encouragement to persecuted believers from the example of the faithful dead (cf. 1 Thessalonians 4:13; Revelation 14:13); 'dead' should be taken literally as in v.5, and the antithesis is between what men do (condemn the flesh) and what God does (give life to the spirit). Robertson notes the difficulty of using 'dead' in two senses so close together, though Jesus did so with 'life' (Matthew 16:25).

Other views: Doddridge, Whitby, Augustine, and Luther take 'the dead' as the spiritually dead — the Gentiles, dead in sins (Ephesians 2:1; Colossians 2:13), to whom the gospel was preached; Clarke, Macknight, and Wesley link it to 3:19, taking 'the dead' as the antediluvians to whom Christ preached by Noah, that though condemned in the flesh by the flood they might live to God in the spirit. The phrase 'judged according to men in the flesh, but live according to God in the spirit' is variously paraphrased but turns on the contrast between human condemnation of the body and divine life given to the soul.

Calvin (with Ellicott) takes it of the literal dead, referring back to 3:19: that death does not hinder Christ from being our defender, his redeeming power extending to the dead — Christ was made known as redeemer to the dead, salvation preached to them. Calvin reads 'judged in the flesh according to men' as a concession (though according to the world's estimation the dead suffer destruction in the flesh and are deemed condemned as to the outward man, yet they live with God in the spirit, Christ quickening them); if Christ's grace once penetrated to the dead, it is not confined to the present life. Ellicott develops a solemn reading that the Antediluvians, judicially destroyed in the flesh, might through the gospel preached to them live in the spirit. All reject the notion that unbelievers are saved after death apart from faith.

debates.question: Who are 'them that are dead' to whom the gospel was preached?

sides.position: Those now dead who heard the gospel while alive and became Christians (judged/killed in the flesh by men, but live to God in the spirit)
The spiritually dead — the Gentiles, dead in sins
The antediluvians, to whom Christ preached by Noah (linking to 3:19)
The literal dead, to whom Christ preached in hades (linking to 3:19)

themes: for this cause was the gospel preached also to them that are dead
that they might be judged according to men in the flesh, but live according to God in the spirit
the gospel preached to the dead — a contested crux
judged in the flesh by men, but alive to God in the spirit

schools.summary: The solemn declaration: 'but the end of all things is at hand (engiken — has drawn near, is approaching): be ye therefore sober (sophronesate — sound-minded, self-controlled, serious), and watch (nepsate — be vigilant, alert) unto prayer' — the consummation of all things near (whether the end of the Jewish state, the end of each man's life, or the final consummation, the nearness the ground for the exhortations that follow); the nearness calling for a sober, self-controlled mind (not given to excess or frivolity, clear-headed seriousness in view of the coming consummation), issuing in watchful prayer (the believer vigilant and prayerful, not drowsy or distracted, keeping alert for prayer in light of the end) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: But the end of all things is at hand: be ye therefore sober, and watch unto prayer
the nearness of the end calls for sobriety and watchful prayer
clear-headed seriousness in view of the consummation
vigilant, alert prayer

schools.summary: The supreme grace: 'and above all things (before all, chiefly) have fervent (ektene — earnest, intense, stretched-out to the utmost) charity (love) among yourselves: for charity shall cover the multitude of sins' — love the chief grace, to be cultivated above all ('fervent' picturing love strained to the utmost, intense, warm, earnest, and persevering, not cool or formal); 'charity shall cover the multitude of sins' (Proverbs 10:12) meaning love covers, overlooks, forgives, and conceals the faults of others — not by atoning for sin, but by being ready to pardon and not expose the many offences of the brethren, love throwing a veil over wrongs rather than publishing them (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: above all things have fervent charity among yourselves
for charity shall cover the multitude of sins
the call to fervent love, which covers a multitude of sins
love forgiving and concealing faults

schools.summary: The practical expression of love: 'use hospitality (be hospitable, welcome and lodge strangers and fellow-believers) one to another without grudging (aneu gongysmou — without murmuring, complaint, or reluctance)' — love issuing in hospitality (the welcoming and entertaining of others, a vital duty in the early church when travelers and persecuted believers needed lodging and depended on the open homes of fellow-Christians, a concrete fruit of the fervent love just commanded); hospitality to be shown gladly and willingly, not grudgingly or with a sense of burden, in the spirit of cheerful, open generosity, not mere reluctant duty (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: Use hospitality one to another without grudging
the call to ungrudging hospitality
love opening the home gladly
cheerful, willing generosity

schools.summary: The principle of mutual service: 'as every man hath received the gift (charisma — a gift of grace, whatever endowment God has given), even so minister (serve with) the same one to another, as good stewards (faithful managers, trustees) of the manifold (poikiles — varied, many-coloured) grace of God' — each believer having received some gift, to use it in serving others (the gift for ministry, not self); every believer having received some gift (none without one, the variety distributed among all, each for the good of the body); the gifts not the believer's own but God's grace entrusted to him, so that he is a steward, accountable to use the varied grace faithfully for others (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: As every man hath received the gift, even so minister the same one to another
as good stewards of the manifold grace of God
the call to use one's gift as a good steward of God's grace
each gift entrusted for the good of others

schools.summary: The rules for speaking and serving, and the doxology: 'if any man speak, let him speak as the oracles (logia — the very utterances, sayings) of God; if any man minister, let him do it as of the ability (ischyos — strength) which God giveth (supplies): that God in all things may be glorified through Jesus Christ, to whom be praise (glory) and dominion for ever and ever. Amen' — the one who speaks in the church to speak as one delivering God's own words (with reverence and fidelity, not his own opinions); the one who serves to do so in the strength God provides (acknowledging that even the power to serve is God's gift); the supreme aim of every gift and service being that God may be glorified through Jesus Christ, the verse closing in a doxology of praise and dominion to God (or Christ) forever (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: if any man speak, let him speak as the oracles of God; if any man minister, let him do it as of the ability which God giveth
that God in all things may be glorified through Jesus Christ
the call to speak and serve so that God may be glorified
God's glory the supreme end of every gift

schools.summary: The tender exhortation: 'beloved, think it not strange (be not surprised, do not regard as alien) concerning the fiery trial (pyrosis — the burning, the ordeal by fire) which is to try (test, prove) you, as though some strange (foreign, unheard-of) thing happened unto you' — the persecution about to come upon them, like a refiner's fire, not to be thought strange or unexpected (trial the normal lot of the believer); the 'fiery trial' the severe persecution that tests and proves faith as fire assays metal (sent to prove and purify, not to destroy); suffering for Christ being normal, foretold, and to be expected, so that to be surprised by it would betray a wrong understanding of the Christian calling (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: think it not strange concerning the fiery trial which is to try you
as though some strange thing happened unto you
the call not to be surprised by the fiery trial
persecution the normal, purifying lot of the believer

schools.summary: The exhortation to paradoxical joy: 'but rejoice, inasmuch as (in the degree that) ye are partakers (sharers) of Christ's sufferings; that, when his glory shall be revealed (at Christ's return in glory), ye may be glad also with exceeding (overflowing, rapturous) joy' — instead of being surprised or dismayed, believers to rejoice that their sufferings make them sharers in Christ's own sufferings (to suffer with him a privilege and ground of joy, a fellowship in his afflictions that leads to fellowship in his glory); present suffering looking forward to the day of his revealed glory, when those who share his sufferings now will share his glory then with surpassing joy (present trial the seed of future rapture) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: rejoice, inasmuch as ye are partakers of Christ's sufferings
when his glory shall be revealed, ye may be glad also with exceeding joy
the call to rejoice as partakers of Christ's sufferings
fellowship in suffering leading to fellowship in glory

schools.summary: The beatitude (echoing Matthew 5:11): 'if ye be reproached (reviled, insulted) for the name of Christ, happy (blessed) are ye; for the spirit of glory and of God (the glorious Spirit of God, the Holy Spirit) resteth (anapauetai — reposes, abides) upon you: on their part (by the persecutors) he is evil spoken of (blasphemed), but on your part (by you) he is glorified' — to be reviled because one bears Christ's name not shame but blessedness (the reproach an honour); the Holy Spirit, the Spirit of glory, resting on the persecuted believer (as the Shekinah glory rested of old, the divine presence and honour with the reproached); the same Christ/Spirit blasphemed by the persecutors glorified by the sufferers, the reproach the world casts turned to God's glory in the believer (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: If ye be reproached for the name of Christ, happy are ye
for the spirit of glory and of God resteth upon you
the blessedness of being reproached for Christ, on whom the Spirit rests
reproach turned to God's glory in the believer

schools.summary: The careful qualification of the blessing: 'but let none of you suffer as a murderer, or as a thief, or as an evildoer (kakopoios — a general term for a criminal), or as a busybody in other men's matters (allotriepiskopos — one who meddles in others' affairs, an officious intermeddler)' — the blessedness belonging only to those who suffer for righteousness, so that if one suffers for actual crimes there is no blessing but deserved punishment; to suffer as a murderer, thief, or evildoer being shameful and just (bringing no honour but disgrace); and alongside the gross crimes the lesser but real fault of meddling in others' business named — the believer not to bring suffering on himself by interfering in others' affairs, even this to be avoided, so that his suffering be for Christ not for sin (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: let none of you suffer as a murderer, or as a thief, or as an evildoer, or as a busybody in other men's matters
the warning not to suffer as a wrongdoer
the blessing only for righteous suffering
even meddling to be avoided

schools.summary: The contrast with v.15: 'yet if any man suffer as a Christian (Christianos — one of the earliest uses of the name, perhaps first a term of reproach applied by enemies, Acts 11:26; 26:28), let him not be ashamed; but let him glorify God on this behalf (en to onomati touto — in this name, or on this account)' — to suffer for bearing the name 'Christian' no disgrace (the believer not to be ashamed of the name or of suffering for it, the name marking one as Christ's); instead of shame, the believer to praise and glorify God for the privilege of suffering as a Christian, the reproach becoming an occasion for thanksgiving (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: if any man suffer as a Christian, let him not be ashamed
but let him glorify God on this behalf
to suffer as a Christian is no shame but ground to glorify God
the honourable name of Christ

schools.summary: The principle: 'for the time is come that judgment (krima — the testing, sifting, chastening judgment) must begin at the house of God (the church, God's household): and if it first begin at us, what shall the end be of them that obey not the gospel of God?' — God's purifying judgment beginning with his own people (the fiery trials coming first upon the church, to sift and purify it); this 'judgment' being the disciplinary, purifying suffering God sends upon his own (not condemnation but chastening, the household tried first as a refiner begins with his own metal); and the solemn inference that if the righteous are subjected to such purifying trial, far more terrible will be the final judgment of the disobedient (the lesser severity to the saints implying a far greater to the lost) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: the time is come that judgment must begin at the house of God
what shall the end be of them that obey not the gospel of God
judgment begins at the house of God
purifying trial for the saints, terrible end for the lost

schools.summary: The proverb cited (Proverbs 11:31, LXX): 'and if the righteous scarcely (molis — with difficulty, through much trial) be saved, where shall the ungodly and the sinner appear (stand, be found)?' — if even the righteous are saved only through great difficulty, trial, and purifying suffering, the ungodly will have no standing at all in the judgment; 'scarcely' meaning not that the righteous are saved by a narrow uncertainty as to the outcome, but that their salvation is attained through much trial (the way hard, the path narrow, salvation costly); the question expecting no answer — if the godly are saved only with difficulty, the wicked have no hope of standing, their case desperate (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: if the righteous scarcely be saved, where shall the ungodly and the sinner appear
if the righteous are scarcely saved, the lost have no standing
salvation attained through much trial
the desperate case of the ungodly

schools.summary: The closing exhortation: 'wherefore let them that suffer according to the will of God (those whose suffering is within God's appointment) commit (paratithesthosan — entrust, deposit, as with a trustee) the keeping of their souls to him in well doing, as unto a faithful (trustworthy, true to his promises) Creator' — the suffering believer to entrust his soul to God for safekeeping (as one deposits a treasure with a faithful guardian), joined with 'well doing' (continuing to do good, not a passive resignation only but an active perseverance in doing right while suffering, faith and obedience together); entrusting his soul 'as unto a faithful Creator' — God, as the one who made and sustains the soul, faithful and able to keep what is committed to him, his faithfulness and creative care the ground of confident entrustment (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: let them that suffer according to the will of God commit the keeping of their souls to him in well doing
as unto a faithful Creator
the call to commit one's soul to the faithful Creator in well doing
entrusting the soul to God while continuing to do good
